Article.ArticleTitle: Menstrual cycles and macrocycles: Science, not socials, is doing the heavy lifting.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Griffith Sports Science, Griffith University, Gold Coast, QLD, Australia.
Female Performance & Health Initiative, Australian Institute of Sport, Canberra, ACT, Australia.

Reference.Citation: Colenso‐Semple, L. M. , McKendry, J. , Lim, C. , & Atherton, P. J. , Wilkinson, D. J. , Smith, K. , Phillips, S. M. (2024). Menstrual cycle phase does not influence muscle protein synthesis or whole‐body myofibrillar proteolysis in response to resistance exercise. Journal of Physiology, 603(5), 1109–1121.

Elliott‐Sale, K. J. , Minahan, C. , de Jonge, X. A. , Ackerman, K. E. , Sipilä, S. , Constantini, N. W. , Lebrun, C. M. , & Hackney, A. C. (2021). Methodological considerations for studies in sport and exercise science with women as participants: A working guide for standards of practice for research on women. Sports Medicine, 51(5), 843–861.

Kissow, J. , Jacobsen, K. J. , Gunnarsson, T. P. , Jessen, S. , & Hostrup, M. (2002). Effects of follicular and luteal phase‐based menstrual cycle resistance training on muscle strength and mass. Sports Medicine, 52(12), 2813–2819.

McNamara, A. , Harris, R. , & Minahan, C. (2022). ‘That time of the month’… for the biggest event of your career! Perception of menstrual cycle on performance of Australian athletes training for the 2020 olympic and paralympic games. British Medical Journal Open Sport & Exercise Medicine, 8, e001300.

Oosthuyse, T. , Strauss, J. A. , & Hackney, A. C. (2023). Understanding the female athlete: molecular mechanisms underpinning menstrual phase differences in exercise metabolism. European Journal of Applied Physiology, 123, 423–450.

Toth, M. J. , Poehlman, E. T. , Matthews, D. E. , Tchernof, A. , & MacCoss, M. J. (2001). Effects of estradiol and progesterone on body composition, protein synthesis, and lipoprotein lipase in rats. American Journal of Physiology‐Endocrinology and Metabolism, 280(3), E496–E501.
